concise:
Ayah 1–8 · linked video: 01. Az-Zalzalah (Ayah 1-8)

Surah Az-Zalzalah: The Earthquake

Ayah 1

إِذَا زُلْزِلَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ زِلْزَالَهَا

When the earth is shaken with its final earthquake.

The surah opens with a powerful word: زلزلة, which describes a violent, intense disturbance or movement. In Arabic, this term can describe the severe beating of a camel to the point where the animal can barely walk straight, stumbling and unable to maintain its balance. This imagery conveys the overwhelming force of what’s being described.

The use of the past tense here indicates a definitive event - this will be the earthquake after which there will be no other. While earthquakes have occurred throughout history, this final one will end them all. This is the ultimate shaking at the end of time.

What makes this ayah particularly striking is the phrase (زِلْزَالَهَا) - “its earthquake.” Normally in Arabic, you might say zulzilat al-ardu zilzālan to add emphasis, but here Allah ﷻ adds an extra pronoun. This construction suggests that this earthquake has always been waiting inside the earth, ready to emerge. It’s as though the earth has been holding this back all along, and it will finally be released.

This connects to another Quranic theme: the earth actually wants to shake violently, and the only force restraining it is Allah’s ﷻ permission. We read elsewhere in the Quran about corruption appearing on land and sea because of human sins. Judgment Day represents the moment when the earth finally reacts to all human sins combined. Natural disasters throughout history have been reactions to some sins, but this will be the earth’s response to everything.

Allah ﷻ describes winds in similar terms - like wild horses whose reins have been released. The calm breeze we feel, the solid ground beneath our feet, the stability of our buildings - we think of these as normal. But the Quran teaches us that this stability is actually the unusual state, maintained only by Allah’s ﷻ mercy. What we call natural disasters are actually closer to the norm, and our everyday peace is the extraordinary mercy. The time will come when Allah ﷻ decides to let the earth have what it has wanted all along: to shake as it was always meant to shake.

Ayah 2

وَأَخْرَجَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ أَثْقَالَهَا

And the earth discharges its burdens.

Notice that Allah ﷻ repeats the word “earth” rather than using a pronoun. The ayah could have simply said “and it brings out its burdens,” but instead says “and the earth brings out its burdens.” This repetition serves to emphasize: Do you realize I’m talking about the same earth you’re walking on right now? This very ground beneath your feet will give out its loads.

The word أَثْقَالَهَا refers to heavy burdens or cargo - things so weighty they require animals to carry them, not something a person could manage alone. This isn’t referring to light loads. The earth has been carrying massive, overwhelming burdens.

The root word thiql appears in Arabic to describe the late stages of pregnancy, when a woman is about to give birth and can barely bear the weight anymore. Using this word to describe the earth creates a powerful image: the earth is pregnant with all of us, with the bodies buried within it, with the secrets and deeds that occurred on its surface. Just as a child grows inside the mother, we rest inside the earth after death, and our deeds - witnessed by the ground itself - are buried along with us.

The earth has been holding these secrets, these pregnancies, and it has had enough. It’s time to give birth. The ground saw where you went, heard what you said, witnessed what you did. Those secrets died with you and were buried, but now the earth can no longer contain them. This is why Allah ﷻ says in another place: “When the earth is stretched out and casts out all that is within it and becomes empty.” It’s like a mother finally relieved after delivery, emptied out, free of the overwhelming burden.

Thiql also describes when depression or debt has overwhelmed someone - when the weight is too much to bear. The earth itself has been saddened, burdened by what has transpired on its surface. Now it will finally let go of what has been overwhelming it.

When we stand for judgment, we may weigh a certain number of pounds or kilograms, but our deeds? They’re like mountains - massive, heavy burdens that the earth has been holding within itself.

Ayah 3

وَقَالَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَا لَهَا

And humanity cries out: “What is wrong with it?”

After these two catastrophic events - the violent shaking and the earth discharging everything within it - the human response is shock and confusion. People will cry out: “What is happening to the earth?”

There’s scholarly discussion about whether al-insān (the human being) here refers specifically to disbelievers or to all of humanity. Some scholars argue that believers will recognize this as Judgment Day and won’t be confused. However, the stronger interpretation, supported by Imam Bint al-Shati among others, is that this refers to all human beings.

She points to Surah Al-Hajj, where Allah ﷻ says: “O humanity, be conscious of your Lord. Indeed, the zalzalah (earthquake) of the Hour is a tremendous thing. On the Day you see it, every nursing mother will forget her nursling, and every pregnant woman will deliver her load.” Allah ﷻ doesn’t say this terror is only for disbelievers - He says everyone. Every mother will drop what she’s carrying. The early events of Judgment Day will be horrifying for all of humanity.

Just as all human beings must taste death - believer and disbeliever alike - there are certain experiences that no one can avoid. This is one of them. It’s not a punishment specific to disbelievers; it’s simply part of the transition from this life to the next. We will all experience this shock. There won’t be secure sections of earth that aren’t shaking, with believers safely observing from protected booths. We’ll all be rattled. We’ll all be in shock.

So humanity will cry out in confusion: What is wrong with the earth?

Ayah 4

يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبَارَهَا

On that Day, it will report its news.

While all of this is happening - the shaking, the disgorging of the dead, the unleashing of secrets and treasures - there’s something even more significant occurring. In the midst of these physical catastrophes, the most shocking event of all: the earth itself will speak.

تُحَدِّث means it will speak, articulate, and elaborate. It will clearly communicate to others about everything that happened on it. The earth will begin testifying about the crimes committed here, the wars fought here, the words spoken here - everything. It witnessed all of it, and now it wants to testify.

The earliest testimony on the Day of Judgment will be the testimony of the earth itself.

This is a sobering thought. Someone about to commit a crime might look around to make sure no one is watching. But who looks down and considers that the earth itself will testify? Every grain of sand, every particle of soil will bear witness.

This extends a theme we find elsewhere in the Quran. Eventually, our own bodies will testify against us. Our limbs will speak, saying, “The One who gave everything the power to speak has given us the power to speak today as well.” But here we’re speaking specifically about the earth’s testimony.

There are narrations that elaborate on this. One states that on the Day of Resurrection, the earth will cry out: “Master, this is what I’ve been keeping inside.” When human beings ask what is wrong with the earth, it will respond: “The way the world used to work is done now. The system of the afterlife has arrived. This is the new order.” Nothing is wrong - this is simply the new reality.

Abu Hassan Al-Ash’ari emphasized that this doesn’t appear to be figurative language, as if the earthquakes themselves constitute the earth’s “speech.” Rather, the ayah suggests the earth will be given an actual voice and will explicitly testify. It will be brought to life in a way we didn’t know about before.

In a narration found in Tirmidhi, when the Prophet ﷺ recited this ayah, he asked his companions: “Do you know what news it will report?” They responded, “Allah and His Messenger know best.” He said: “It will testify about every male and female slave regarding whatever they did on its surface. It will say: ‘He did this on that day,’ and ‘She did this on that day.’ This will be its news.”

This concept isn’t unprecedented in the Quran. Allah ﷻ makes Hell itself speak on Judgment Day. The Fire will say, “Is there any more?” It will call to people trying to flee from it. People will hear its roar and snarl like a lion’s growl. Several elements of the unseen will be given speech on Judgment Day, and the earth is clearly among them.

Ayah 5

بِأَنَّ رَبَّكَ أَوْحَىٰ لَهَا

Because your Lord will have inspired it.

All of this testimony from the earth happens because its Master - Allah ﷻ - has inspired it to speak.

There’s a grammatical subtlety here worth noting. Throughout the Quran, when the verb (أَوْحَىٰ - “to inspire” or “to reveal”) is used, it consistently comes with the preposition (إِلَىٰ - “to”). For example, “Your Lord inspired to the bee.” But here, instead of awḥā ilā, Allah ﷻ says awḥā lahā - using the preposition (لَ) instead.

This grammatical shift carries two significant implications. First, it combines the meanings of both inspiration and permission. Allah ﷻ didn’t just inspire the earth - He also gave it explicit permission to speak. By using this construction, Allah ﷻ compressed multiple meanings into a single expression.

Second, the preposition ilā suggests some distance between the one giving instruction and the one receiving it. But lām removes that distance and creates direct connection. Notice throughout this surah: there are no angels mentioned as intermediaries, no armies executing commands. It’s Rabbuka - your Lord - directly inspiring the earth, and the earth itself directly receiving and complying with His command.

There’s no gap between Allah’s ﷻ instruction and the earth’s obedience. The two have been brought intimately close, suggesting the direct presence and arrival of Allah ﷻ Himself on that Day. The direct connection between Allah ﷻ and the earth has been established through the use of lahā.

This is the Day when Allah ﷻ will inspire the earth to speak, to testify, to reveal everything it has witnessed.

Ayah 6

يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ ٱلنَّاسُ أَشْتَاتًا لِّيُرَوْا۟ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ

On that Day people will proceed in separate groups to be shown their deeds.

Now that the earth’s testimony has begun, the next scene unfolds. The word (يَصْدُرُ) is particularly interesting. Ṣadr refers to departing from a watering place in the desert. When travelers journey through the desert searching for water, they eventually find it, replenish themselves, and then move on. The act of leaving that water source and continuing the journey is called ṣadr.

Why does Allah ﷻ use this imagery? He’s describing our entire worldly life as a brief stop at a watering hole during a much longer journey. Long before you and I came to this earth - since the time of Adam (AS) - our souls have existed. Countless years passed, and then Allah ﷻ took your soul, placed it inside your mother’s womb, and gave you a little bit of provision, a little water for a brief time on this earth. This was just a rest stop. Now it’s time to move on, because the journey continues to the next station.

Yaṣdurun-nāsu - people are done with this temporary stop. The time has come to move forward.

But while at this temporary stop, everyone shared the same planet. They shared neighborhoods, families. Believers and disbelievers lived in the same households. Remember the previous surah’s message about how people wouldn’t be separated until clear proof came. Now, as people move on from this journey, they won’t be together anymore.

(أَشْتَاتًا) - they will be broken up into separate groups, heading in different directions. This picks up a theme from earlier surahs: “Your efforts are diverse.” Depending on the kinds of efforts your life was made up of, that’s the group you’ll belong to, heading in a particular direction based on your actions.

People will be categorized - not by race, not by height or color or language or nationality - but by deeds. The murderer from here and the murderer from Australia and the murderer from another era will be grouped together. The one who prayed regularly, the one who gave charity, the forgiver - wherever they were from, whatever generation they belonged to, whatever society they lived in, whatever prophet they followed - they’ll be grouped together by category of deed.

Li-yuraw a’mālahum - so they may be shown their deeds. That’s one kind of categorization. The Quran mentions other categorizations too: “The Day We will call every people by their leader” - meaning by the prophets they followed or the false gods they worshipped. There may be multiple stages of categorization on Judgment Day, but the central point is clear: people will move on from this life and be separated into groups based on their deeds so they may witness what they’ve done.

Ayah 7

فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُۥ

So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it.

We arrive at the concluding message of this remarkable surah, and notice how the tone shifts. After all the terrifying imagery - the violent shaking, the earth giving birth to its dead, the shocking testimony - Allah ﷻ now turns to something hopeful, something merciful.

(مِثْقَال) means the equivalent weight of something, like a gram or kilogram or pound - a standard by which weight is measured. Allah ﷻ says whoever may have done the worth of a (ذَرَّة).

What is a dharrah? If you’re sitting by a window on a sunny day and you see tiny particles of dust floating in the beam of light, barely visible - one of those specks is a dharrah. These are particles almost impossible to see, let alone weigh or assign value to.

Allah ﷻ is saying that human beings will be shocked on Judgment Day when they’re shown good deeds they never even remembered doing. Things they didn’t even consider good deeds - Allah ﷻ counted them, recorded them, and now they will see them.

This is the second time in the surah we’re told about human shock. The first shock was when the earth was shaking and people cried out, “What is wrong with it?” The second shock is this: when they see that a speck - a flying particle of dust - was counted and valued.

Notice where dust comes from: the earth. In the beginning of the surah, the earth gave up its heavy burdens. Now, by the end, not only are the heavy burdens significant - even a speck is significant. The word thiql (heavy weight) was used twice for the earth’s loads, and now the same root appears here: mithqāla dharratin - the weight of a speck.

It’s remarkable mercy from Allah ﷻ that when we think of Judgment Day, we usually think of our bad deeds first. This entire surah has been frightening, overwhelming. But by the end, before mentioning the bad deeds, Allah ﷻ says: None of your good deeds will be wasted. Not a single one will go unrecorded. Even if it’s so small it was impossible to measure in this world, it will be counted and measured on that Day.

But here’s the crucial question: How can something impossible to measure in this world have value on Judgment Day?

The answer lies in understanding that the scales of the two worlds are completely different. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that actions are judged by intentions. But that’s not how this world works - this world runs on results. If an employee did everything right but made no sales, they lose their job regardless of their intentions. If a charity feeds fewer people, its graphs go down, even if the workers sacrificed everything.

The world measures what it can see. The fundraiser recognizes who gave fifty thousand dollars but overlooks the person who gave five dollars - even though that person gave everything they had. In this world, it only added five dollars to the total. But in the next world, those five dollars might outweigh all the thousands donated by the wealthy.

The scales of that Day are different because they’re based on the level of sacrifice you made, the level of difficulty you faced. It may be that the fifty thousand was worth nothing, or worth less than the fifty cents given by someone else.

Think about the scholars who transmitted this religion to us. We know their names, but we rarely know much about their mothers, their fathers, the people who influenced them. The love and sacrifice of a mother who raised a great scholar - the world may not even know her name, but that woman is reaping immense rewards from the entire Ummah because of what she did. There are people in the spotlight and people behind the scenes, people who are prominent and people who are invisible.

This is why Allah ﷻ calls this worldly life a game, a play, a deception. Someone looks like a king, someone looks like a beggar. But on Judgment Day, the beggar may look like a king and the king may look like a beggar. Everything will flip.

Fa-man ya’mal mithqāla dharratin khayran yarah - whoever does even the weight of an atom of khayr (good) will see it. The word (يَعْمَلْ) implies conscious action, something done with intention. Even if it’s worth so little - even if our prayer was faulty, our mind wandering, our focus broken - that one percent of sincerity in it, Allah ﷻ will count it. He’ll give it to you.

This is why at the end of every prayer, we say Rabbanā taqabbal minnā - “Our Lord, accept from us.” It’s the supplication of Ibrahim (AS) after building the Ka’bah, and how much more valid is it for us! Our worship is broken, flawed, full of problems. But whatever patch job we’ve managed to offer, we ask Allah ﷻ: please accept it from us.

Notice too that Allah ﷻ mentions khayr (good) first, before mentioning evil in the next ayah. This is deliberate and merciful. Judgment Day is terrifying, but Allah ﷻ wants you to see your good deeds first. When you go to court or present your case, if you start with a mistake, the entire impression suffers. But if you start well and then have some errors, the overall impression is better. Allah ﷻ wants to boost your confidence from the beginning: Let’s highlight your good deeds first.

Ayah 8

وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُۥ

And whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it.

The parallel structure continues. Just as every speck of good will be seen, every speck of (شَرّ) will also be seen.

Khayr doesn’t just mean “good” - it means “better,” the better choice. Sharr doesn’t just mean “evil” - it means “worse,” the worse choice, and it also carries the meaning of harm. You constantly face choices: Should you say the better thing or the worse thing? Should you do the better action or the worse action? When you chose the harmful option, when you chose what caused damage - sharr encompasses harm to yourself and harm to others - that too will be counted.

Wa-man ya’mal mithqāla dharratin sharran yarah - whoever did an atom’s weight of evil, of harm, will absolutely see it.

Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “will be punished for it” or “will be judged for it.” He just says “will see it.” The ayah ends with seeing, leaving the outcome open-ended. You’ll see what you did. Now what? The surah doesn’t answer that question.

That answer comes in the next surah. Once you’ve seen everything - once you know that every microscopic particle has been counted - then comes the weighing. Fa-man thaqulat mawāzīnuh - whoever’s scales are heavy. The picture will be completed in the next surah. And once it’s weighed, it will be judged: Is it worth Paradise or Hellfire?

But right now, we’re left with the shock of simply looking at what we’ve done. Just looking. The full weight of the realization that nothing was hidden, nothing was lost, nothing escaped Allah’s ﷻ record.

As we reflect on this surah’s structure, we can see it’s organized in three parts:

The opening describes the earth unleashing its secrets, and human beings responding in shock. The conclusion mirrors this: human beings are in shock again, but this time not over the entire earth - rather, over every speck of dirt, every particle of the earth, every atom’s weight of their deeds.

In the middle of the surah stands the central theme: Yawma’idhin tuḥaddithu akhbārahā - the Day when the earth will speak its testimony, when Allah ﷻ will have inspired it, when people will be separated into groups based on their deeds to be shown what they’ve done.

This brief surah, only eight ayahs, captures the terror, the justice, the precision, and ultimately the mercy of the Day of Judgment. It reminds us that we walk on a witness, we live on a recorder, and one day all secrets will be revealed. But it also promises us that no good will be wasted, no matter how small, no matter how overlooked in this world.

May Allah ﷻ protect us on the Day of Judgment. May He make our good deeds heavy and our bad deeds light and forgiven.
deep:
Ayah 1–8 · linked video: 01. Az-Zalzalah (Ayah 1-8)

Root Word Analysis

The word zalzalah (زَلْزَلَة) derives from the root z-l-z-l (ز-ل-ز-ل), which conveys intense, violent movement or disturbance. In Arabic, zalzalah specifically refers to a powerful earthquake or shaking. The verb form zalzala means to shake violently, and classical Arabic uses this term when describing something being shaken so severely that it loses stability—like a camel beaten so harshly it can barely walk straight anymore.

The phrase zalzalataha (زَلْزَلَتَهَا) is grammatically significant. Normally, you would say zalzalat al-ard (the earth was shaken), and for emphasis zalzalat al-ard zilzalaha (the earth was completely shaken). However, the Quran uses zalzalat al-ardu zilzalaha—adding the pronoun ha (her/its) at the end. This additional pronoun suggests that this is THE earthquake the earth has been waiting to unleash all along. It’s not just any shaking; it’s the earthquake that was always meant to happen, as if it’s been hiding inside the earth, finally given permission to emerge.

Athqalaha (أَثْقَالَهَا) comes from the root th-q-l (ث-ق-ل), meaning heaviness, burden, or weight. The word thiqal refers to heavy cargo or loads that require animals to carry—things too heavy for humans to bear alone. In the Quran, athqal appears only three times, and here it describes the earth’s overwhelming burdens. The word also carries the meaning of ikhtiqal—when something has become so overwhelming that it can no longer be borne, like the late stages of pregnancy when a woman is about to give birth.

The verb akhrajat (أَخْرَجَتْ) from the root kh-r-j (خ-ر-ج) means to bring out, expel, or release. When paired with athqalaha, it creates a powerful image: the earth is pregnant with the dead and their deeds, and now it’s time to give birth—to release everything it has been holding inside.

Tuhaddithu (تُحَدِّثُ) comes from h-d-th (ح-د-ث), meaning to speak, narrate, or relate events. The Form II verb haddatha means to speak in detail, to elaborate. Akhbaraha (أَخْبَارَهَا) from kh-b-r (خ-ب-ر) means news, events, or reports. Together, tuhaddithu akhbaraha means the earth will speak explicitly about everything that happened on it—it will testify, narrate, and detail all the events it witnessed.

Awha (أَوْحَىٰ) from w-h-y (و-ح-ي) means to inspire, reveal, or communicate directly. The unusual use of laha (لَهَا) instead of the more common ilayha (إِلَيْهَا) is grammatically significant. Normally, awha takes the preposition ila (to), but here Allah uses lam (ل), which serves to remove distance and establish direct connection. This suggests Allah is speaking directly to the earth without intermediaries—no angels, no gap, just direct divine communication. The lam also combines meanings, suggesting both inspiration and permission were granted simultaneously.

Yashduru (يَصْدُرُ) comes from s-d-r (ص-د-ر), meaning to proceed, return from, or move away from a water source. In desert contexts, sadar specifically means to leave a watering place after drinking and continue the journey. Ashtata (أَشْتَاتًا) from sh-t-t (ش-ت-ت) means scattered, dispersed, or in separate groups. The phrase yasduru an-nasu ashtata beautifully captures that humanity’s time at this temporary worldly “watering hole” is over, and now people are moving on to the next stage—but this time broken into separate groups based on their deeds.

Mithqal (مِثْقَال) from th-q-l (ث-ق-ل) means the equivalent weight or measure of something. It’s a unit of measurement, like a gram or milligram. Dharrah (ذَرَّة) refers to the tiniest speck—the dust particles visible when sunlight streams through a window. Classical Arabic defined it as al-habba (the grain) that floats in the air on a sunny day. These particles are so small they’re almost impossible to see, let alone weigh.

The repetition of mithqala dharratin (the weight of a speck) for both khayran (good) and sharran (evil/harm) emphasizes divine precision in judgment. The word khayran doesn’t just mean good—it means better, something superior. Sharran doesn’t just mean evil—it means worse, harm, or something inferior. This suggests constant choices between better and worse options throughout life.

The verb yarahu (يَرَهُ) from r-a-y (ر-أ-ي) means to see. Allah doesn’t say people will be punished or judged yet—just that they will SEE. This leaves the outcome mysteriously open-ended, creating anticipation for what comes next in Surah Al-Qari’ah, which will describe the weighing and final judgment.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

The Surah opens with idha (إِذَا)—“when”—which in Arabic creates a sense of certainty about a future event. This isn’t in (if), which would suggest possibility; idha conveys inevitability. The earthquake WILL happen; it’s not a matter of if, but when.

The passive construction zulzilat (زُلْزِلَتْ) is grammatically significant. Who is shaking the earth? The passive voice leaves the agent unmentioned initially, creating mystery and emphasizing the event itself over the doer. Later, bi-anna rabbaka awha laha reveals that Allah جل جلاله is the One granting permission, but even this is indirect—He “inspires” the earth to act.

The repetition of al-ard (الْأَرْض) in the first two verses is a powerful rhetorical device. Normally, Arabic avoids repetition by using pronouns for subsequent references. You would expect: idha zulzilat al-ardu zilzalaha wa-akhrajat athqalaha (when the earth is shaken… and IT brings out…). Instead, Allah says wa-akhrajat al-ardu athqalaha—repeating “the earth” again. This repetition forces the listener to visualize: “This earth beneath your feet RIGHT NOW—this same earth will bring out its burdens.” It’s a wake-up call, grounding (literally) the eschatological in present reality.

The addition of the feminine pronoun ha in zilzalaha creates what grammarians call tawkid (emphasis). The structure moves from general to specific to emphatic: shaken → its shaking → THE shaking it was always meant to have. This grammatical intensification mirrors the physical intensity of the event.

The phrase bi-anna rabbaka awha laha employs bi-anna (because/on account of the fact that) to provide causation. But notice the word order: rabbaka (YOUR master) comes before the verb awha (inspired). This fronting emphasizes the relationship between the earth and its Creator—highlighting divine sovereignty and the earth’s submission.

The unusual preposition switch from ila to lam in awha laha (instead of awha ilayha) is what Arabic grammarians call tadmin (meaning-inclusion). When one verb’s preposition is used with another verb, both meanings combine. So awha (inspired) with lam includes meanings of both inspiration AND speaking directly to, plus granting permission. This rhetorical compression packs multiple meanings into fewer words—a hallmark of Quranic eloquence.

The passive construction continues with qala al-insanu (the human will say). Notice this isn’t sayaqulu (he will say) in future tense with sawfa, nor is it yaqulu (he says) in simple present. The past tense qala used for a future event is called al-madi bi-ma’na al-mustaqbal—past tense expressing future certainty, as if it’s already happened. This makes the Day of Judgment feel imminent, inevitable, and already unfolding.

Ma laha (what is wrong with it?) is a rhetorical question expressing shock and bewilderment. The structure ma + lam typically indicates questioning about a state or condition. The human doesn’t ask “what is happening?” but “what is WRONG with it?”—suggesting the earth’s behavior seems abnormal, even though this is actually the earth’s true nature being unleashed.

The phrase yawma’idhin tuhaddithu akhbaraha features the demonstrative time marker idhin (إِذٍ) attached to yawma (يَوْمَ), creating “on that day”—pointing specifically to the Day mentioned earlier. This creates textual cohesion, linking the earth’s testimony to its shaking.

Tuhaddithu is Form II, which often indicates intensity or causation. It’s not just hadatha (it spoke), but haddithu—it will speak extensively, elaborate, detail, narrate at length. The object akhbaraha (its news/events) is indefinite, suggesting multiplicity—all the events, countless occurrences.

The verb yasduru is in Form I, but its meaning of “departing from a watering place” is specialized pastoral vocabulary. Using this specific term rather than generic words for “leaving” or “going forth” creates a metaphorical framework: this life is merely a brief stop for refreshment during a long journey. We’re not residents here; we’re travelers passing through.

Ashtata (أَشْتَاتًا) is the plural of shatt, meaning scattered or dispersed. As a hal (circumstantial descriptor), it describes the STATE in which people will proceed—not as a unified mass, but scattered into distinct groups. This grammatical construction emphasizes the separation that will occur.

Li-yuraw a’malahum (that they may be shown their deeds) uses the subjunctive li-yura indicating purpose. The passive voice again is significant—they will be SHOWN, not they will see. Someone (Allah) will show them. This construction implies divine agency in the revelation of deeds.

The parallel structure of the final two verses creates powerful symmetry:

Fa-man ya’mal mithqala dharratin khayran yarahu Wa-man ya’mal mithqala dharratin sharran yarahu

(So whoever does the weight of a speck of good, he will see it And whoever does the weight of a speck of evil, he will see it)

The exact grammatical parallelism—fa-man ya’mal… yarahu / wa-man ya’mal… yarahu—creates rhythmic balance and emphasizes divine justice: perfect symmetry in accountability. The repetition of mithqala dharratin for both good and evil reinforces that the same precise standard applies to both.

The verb yarahu (يَرَهُ) is remarkably restrained. After all this buildup—earthquakes, testimonies, groupings—Allah doesn’t say people will be rewarded or punished yet. Just yarahu—he will SEE it. This rhetorical restraint creates anticipation and leaves the outcome tantalizingly open-ended, compelling the listener to continue to the next Surah (Al-’Adiyat and Al-Qari’ah) for resolution.

The Surah’s structure itself is rhetorically sophisticated: it begins and ends with human shock. First shock: ma laha (what’s wrong with it?) when seeing the earth’s earthquake. Final shock: seeing every microscopic deed recorded. This envelope structure (inclusio) creates thematic unity—from the macro (earth’s upheaval) to the micro (specks of dust), human beings are in a state of astonishment at divine precision and power.

Historical & Biblical Context

The concept of the earth testifying on Judgment Day has precedent in earlier Islamic teachings. According to a narration in Sunan Ibn Majah, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم taught that on the Day of Resurrection, the earth will cry out to Allah: “Master, this is what I’ve been keeping inside.” When humans ask ma laha (what is wrong with it?), the earth will respond: “The way the world used to work is done now. The order of the Akhirah has arrived.”

Another hadith found in Sunan al-Tirmidhi (graded hasan) relates that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم recited this ayah about the earth speaking its news and asked the Companions (RA): “Do you know what events it will talk about?” They replied, “Allah and His Messenger know best.” He صلى الله عليه وسلم said: “On that day the earth will testify against every slave, male and female, about whatever they did on the surface of this earth. It will say, ‘He did this on that day, and she did this on that day.’ This is how it will speak—this is its news.”

The idea of creation testifying against or for humanity appears in multiple places in the Quran. In Surah Ya-Sin (36:65), Allah says: “al-yawma nakhtimu ’ala afwahihim wa-tukalimuna aydihim wa-tashhadu arjuluhum” (Today We seal their mouths, and their hands will speak to Us, and their feet will testify). In Surah Fussilat (41:20-21), those being testified against will question their own limbs: “Lima shahidtum ’alayna?” (Why did you testify against us?). The limbs reply: “Antaqana Allahu alladhi antaqa kulla shay’” (Allah, Who gave speech to everything, has given us speech).

The Quran describes Hell itself speaking on the Day of Judgment. In Surah Qaf (50:30), Hell asks: “Hal min mazid” (Is there any more?). In Surah Al-Furqan (25:12), it says: “Idha ra’at-hum min makanin ba’idin sami’u laha taghayyudhan wa-zafiira” (When it sees them from a distant place, they will hear its raging and roaring). This establishes that giving speech to inanimate creation on Judgment Day is consistent with other Quranic teachings.

The imagery of earthquakes as signs of the Hour appears throughout hadith literature. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم described the major signs of the Last Day, including widespread earthquakes that will devastate the earth. However, this Surah describes THE final earthquake—zalzalataha—the one the earth has been restraining all along.

The concept that natural disasters are sometimes responses to human sin appears in Surah Ar-Rum (30:41): “Dhahara al-fasadu fi al-barri wa-al-bahri bima kasabat aydi an-nas” (Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what people’s hands have earned). This establishes that the earth reacts to human moral failure even in this world, but Judgment Day is when it will react to ALL sins collectively.

Biblical parallels exist to the earth witnessing human deeds. In Genesis, after Cain kills Abel, Allah says to Cain: “The voice of your brother’s blood cries out to Me from the ground” (Genesis 4:10). The earth itself bears witness to the first murder. In the New Testament, Jesus (AS) says: “I tell you, if these [disciples] were silent, the very stones would cry out” (Luke 19:40). However, the Quranic concept goes further—not just that the earth COULD speak, but that it definitively WILL speak on Judgment Day.

The description of people being grouped by their deeds connects to the Quranic theme of being assembled with whom or what you worshipped. In Surah Al-Isra (17:71), Allah says: “Yawma nad’u kulla unasin bi-imamihim” (The Day We will call every people by their leader/imam). This refers to people being grouped by their prophets or false gods—Ibrahim (AS) with his followers, Musa (AS) with his, Isa (AS) with his, and idolaters with their idols.

No specific asbab al-nuzul (circumstances of revelation) are authentically reported for this Surah, though classical scholars note it is Madinan according to some and Makkan according to others. The style and content suggest it could fit either period, as eschatological themes appear throughout the Quran’s revelation.

The weighing of deeds to the precision of a dharrah (speck) connects to the Quranic concept of the Mizan (the Scale). In Surah Al-A’raf (7:8-9), Allah describes: “Wa-al-waznu yawma’idhin al-haqq” (The weighing that Day is the truth). Those whose scales are heavy will succeed, and those whose scales are light will have lost themselves. This Surah adds the detail that even particles invisible to the human eye will be placed on those scales.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم taught about the value of small deeds in multiple hadiths. He said: “Do not belittle any good deed, even meeting your brother with a cheerful face” (Sahih Muslim). Another hadith describes a prostitute who gave water to a thirsty dog and was forgiven, while a woman who starved a cat was punished (Sahih Bukhari). These teachings reinforce that deeds which seem insignificant in this world—a speck’s worth—can have immense consequences in the Hereafter.

Coherence

Surah Az-Zalzalah is part of a four-surah cluster toward the end of the Quran: Az-Zalzalah (99), Al-’Adiyat (100), Al-Qari’ah (101), and At-Takathur (102). These four surahs form an alternating pattern: Judgment-Greed-Judgment-Greed. Az-Zalzalah (99) focuses exclusively on Judgment Day. Al-’Adiyat (100) focuses on human greed and ingratitude (innahu li-hubbi al-khayri la-shadid—indeed he is intense in his love of wealth). Al-Qari’ah (101) returns to Judgment Day with more details about the weighing of deeds. At-Takathur (102) focuses again on greed (alhakum at-takathur—competition in accumulation distracts you).

This pattern suggests that the remedy for greed is remembrance of the Day of Judgment. The formula being depicted is: when you remember that every speck of good and evil will be weighed, you are less likely to be consumed by competition for worldly accumulation. The placement is pedagogically deliberate.

Az-Zalzalah and Al-Qari’ah specifically complete each other thematically. Az-Zalzalah ends with people SEEING their deeds (yarahu) without describing the outcome. Al-Qari’ah picks up exactly where Az-Zalzalah leaves off, describing what happens AFTER the deeds are seen: fa-amma man thaqulat mawazinuhu fa-huwa fi ’ishatin radiyah (As for the one whose scales are heavy, he will be in a pleasant life). Wa-amma man khaffat mawazinuhu fa-ummuhu hawiyah (But as for the one whose scales are light, his refuge will be Hawiyah—the Abyss). This is called iltifa’ (complementarity)—the surahs complete one another’s message.

Within Az-Zalzalah itself, the structure demonstrates perfect thematic unity:

Opening (1-2): The earth shakes and releases its burdens—MACRO shock Middle (3-6): Human reaction, earth’s testimony, divine inspiration, people grouped by deeds Closing (7-8): Individual deeds weighed down to the speck—MICRO shock

The movement from macro to micro mirrors the movement from collective judgment to individual accountability. The whole earth shakes (collective event), then the earth speaks about what happened on it (communal testimony), then people are shown their individual deeds (personal reckoning), then even the tiniest individual actions are revealed (ultimate precision).

The repetition of shock creates an envelope structure (inclusio): the human says ma laha (what’s wrong with it?) in verse 3, and the implicit question at the end is “How can something this small matter?” Both are expressions of human astonishment at divine power and precision.

The word ard (earth) appears explicitly in verses 1 and 2, and implicitly in verses 7-8 through the image of dharrah (dust particle from the earth). This creates thematic bookends—from earth’s massive shaking to earth’s tiniest particles, both will testify.

The Surah connects to themes from earlier Meccan surahs about Judgment Day. Surah Al-Waqi’ah (56:4-6) describes: “Idha rujjati al-ardu rajja, wa-bussati al-jibalu bassa, fa-kanat haba’an munbaththan” (When the earth is shaken with a violent shaking, and the mountains are crumbled completely, and become scattered dust). Az-Zalzalah adds the detail that the earth will SPEAK about what it witnessed.

The theme of nature being restrained until the Hour appears in Surah At-Takwir (81:1-6): “Idha ash-shamsu kuwwirat, wa-idha an-nujumu inkadarat…” (When the sun is wrapped up, when the stars fall…). Az-Zalzalah specifically focuses on the EARTH’S role, while At-Takwir focuses on the HEAVENS’ role. Together, they paint a complete picture of cosmic dissolution.

The concept of the earth giving up what’s inside appears in Surah Al-Inshiqaq (84:3-4): “Wa-idha al-ardu muddat, wa-alqat ma fiha wa-takhallat” (When the earth is stretched out and has cast out what is in it and become empty). Az-Zalzalah specifies that among what the earth casts out are its athqal (heavy burdens)—the dead and the record of their deeds.

The phrase li-yuraw a’malahum (that they may be shown their deeds) connects to Surah Al-Kahf (18:49): “Wa-wudi’a al-kitabu fa-tara al-mujrimina mushfiqina mimma fihi” (And the record will be placed, and you will see the criminals fearful of what is in it). The “showing” described in Az-Zalzalah is the OPENING of the book of deeds described throughout the Quran.

The word ashtata (scattered groups) links to Surah Al-An’am (6:65): “Aw yalbisakum shiya’an” (Or He could divide you into sects). On Judgment Day, the divisions won’t be by tribe, nation, or race, but by DEEDS—a complete re-categorization of humanity.

The imagery of weighing deeds connects backward to Surah Al-A’raf (7:8-9), forward to Surah Al-Qari’ah (101:6-9), and to the hadith literature about the Mizan (the Scale). Az-Zalzalah contributes the crucial detail that the scale is precise down to a dharrah—meaning NO deed is too small to be weighed.

The final two verses (fa-man ya’mal…) create a universal principle that applies to all the ethical teachings of the Quran. Every command to do good and avoid evil carries the implicit reminder: yarahu—you will see it. This makes Az-Zalzalah a summary of divine justice, a closing argument for moral accountability that reverberates throughout the entire Quranic message.

Psychological, Political & Social

The psychological impact of this Surah begins with SHOCK. The opening image—idha zulzilat al-ardu zilzalaha—jolts the listener into attention. Human psychology tends toward complacency; we assume tomorrow will be like today, that the earth beneath our feet will remain stable. The Quran disrupts this false sense of security. The earth you walk on is not neutral—it’s WAITING to shake. This creates a healthy psychological tension: don’t get too comfortable.

The human response—qala al-insanu ma laha—reveals a deep psychological truth: when our stable reality suddenly shifts, our first reaction is bewilderment and denial. “What’s wrong with it?” suggests the human expects the earth to remain predictable and controllable. We’ve built our entire civilization on the assumption that the ground won’t open beneath us. Judgment Day shatters that illusion. This verse captures the psychological vertigo of having your worldview collapse.

The earth speaking—tuhaddithu akhbaraha—introduces a profound psychological dimension: You are NEVER truly alone. Even when no human witnesses your actions, the ground beneath you is watching, listening, recording. This awareness should produce muraqabah (consciousness of being watched). If you knew the walls could testify, would you sin in private? The earth’s testimony psychologically eliminates the illusion of privacy in wrongdoing.

The phrase bi-anna rabbaka awha laha (because your Lord inspired it) shifts the psychological frame from fear of the earth to accountability before Allah. It’s not random; it’s divinely orchestrated. This creates a psychology of PURPOSE—everything that happens on Judgment Day is meaningful, intentional, just. This counters the modern anxiety of living in a meaningless universe. Your life mattered. Your choices mattered. Even the earth cared about what you did.

Yasduru an-nasu ashtata (people will proceed in scattered groups) reveals the SOCIAL dissolution that occurs on Judgment Day. In this life, we find identity in family, tribe, nation, ethnicity. But those social bonds will be severed. You won’t be with your family unless they earned it through deeds. The disbeliever father and believer son will be separated. This is psychologically devastating for those who assumed blood ties would protect them, but liberating for those who were oppressed by their families for choosing faith.

The purpose clause li-yuraw a’malahum (that they may be shown their deeds) has immense psychological weight. Human beings engage in constant self-deception. We rationalize our sins, minimize our faults, exaggerate our virtues. Judgment Day removes all self-deception. You will see EXACTLY what you did, with perfect clarity, no filters, no excuses. This is psychologically terrifying for the guilty and vindicating for the righteous who doubted themselves.

Fa-man ya’mal mithqala dharratin khayran yarahu introduces a psychology of HOPE and MOTIVATION. No good deed is wasted. That smile you gave when you were exhausted. That charity you gave when no one saw. That moment you controlled your anger. That time you forgave when you could have taken revenge. ALL of it counted. This combats the psychological despair of thinking “what difference does it make?”

Conversely, wa-man ya’mal mithqala dharratin sharran yarahu creates a psychology of ACCOUNTABILITY and deterrence. That “small” lie. That “harmless” backbiting. That moment of arrogance. That brief glance at the haram. ALL of it counted too. This verse psychologically closes the loopholes we create: “It’s not a big deal,” “Everyone does it,” “It’s just a small thing.” No—it’s a dharrah, and you WILL see it.

The social implications are revolutionary. In this world, we measure success by visible, quantifiable results: wealth, fame, followers, degrees, titles. But mithqala dharratin establishes a completely different metric. The powerful CEO who exploited workers and the poor janitor who prayed tahajjud—on Judgment Day, the scales might completely flip. This should produce a profound SOCIAL HUMILITY: don’t judge people by external measures; you don’t know whose deeds weigh more.

The Surah challenges POLITICAL power structures. Tyrants rule by creating fear and projecting invincibility. But idha zulzilat al-ardu—when the earth itself rebels against injustice, no throne will stand. Tuhaddithu akhbaraha—the earth will testify against the oppressor. Every drop of blood spilled, every cry of the oppressed that soaked into the ground—the earth remembers. This empowers the oppressed: your suffering is not forgotten, and the ground itself will testify for you.

The phrase rabbuka (YOUR Lord) in verse 5 creates psychological intimacy. Not rabbuha (its Lord), but rabbuka (YOUR Lord). Allah is reminding the listener: this isn’t just cosmic drama; it’s PERSONAL. Your Master is the One orchestrating all of this. This should produce both khawf (awe) and raja’ (hope)—He is aware of you individually.

The social dimension of ashtata (scattered groups) also suggests PEER INFLUENCE matters eternally. You won’t just be judged individually; you’ll be grouped with those who influenced you or whom you influenced. The scholar and his students together. The gang leader and his followers together. The righteous friend who kept you on track—you’ll be in their group. This creates a social ethic: choose your companions carefully; you may be resurrected with them.

Psychologically, the Surah ends on SUSPENSE. Yarahu (he will see it)—but then what? No reward or punishment is mentioned yet. This open-ending creates psychological anticipation that drives the listener to the next Surah (Al-Qari’ah), where the consequences are revealed: fa-amma man thaqulat mawazinuhu… (As for one whose scales are heavy…). The psychological technique is powerful: leave them thinking, wanting to know more.

The image of the earth being “pregnant” with our deeds (athqalaha) creates a psychology of INEVITABILITY. Just as a pregnancy must result in birth, our deeds must result in consequences. You can’t keep them hidden forever. The earth is about to “give birth” to everything you thought was buried and forgotten. This produces the prophetic warning: don’t assume your secrets died with you—they’re alive, waiting to be born into the courtroom of Judgment Day.

From a BEHAVIORAL PSYCHOLOGY perspective, the Surah functions as both MOTIVATION (your good deeds count) and DETERRENT (your bad deeds count). The symmetry of the final verses—same structure for good and evil—creates balanced psychological conditioning: be eager to do good (positive reinforcement) and cautious about evil (negative reinforcement).

The social ethic of mithqala dharratin combats UTILITARIANISM and CONSEQUENTIALISM. Modern ethics often judge actions by their visible outcomes: “Did it work?” “Did it produce results?” But Islam judges by INTENTION and EFFORT, even if results are invisible. The person who tried to help but failed still gets reward. The person who succeeded in evil through deception still gets punishment. This creates a more HUMANE social ethic—we’re not just judged by outcomes we can’t always control, but by the sincerity and effort we CAN control.

Finally, the psychological movement of the Surah—from global catastrophe (earthquake) to individual accountability (specks of dust)—mirrors the journey every soul must make: from being part of the masses to standing ALONE before Allah. This produces healthy INDIVIDUALISM (your deeds are your own; no one can carry your burden) while maintaining COLLECTIVISM (the earth testifies about communal events; we share in the legacy of our societies).

Quranic Wisdom

The profound wisdom of idha zulzilat al-ardu zilzalaha is that this life is not the NORMAL state of creation—it’s the UNUSUAL mercy. We think earthquakes are disasters and calm ground is normal. Allah reveals: the earth WANTS to shake; I’m the One holding it back. This inverts our entire perspective. Every stable moment is a GIFT, not a given. The ground beneath your feet staying still is not the default—it’s divine mercy in action. When you realize this, you stop taking stability for granted and recognize every peaceful day as ni’mah (blessing).

The wisdom of wa-akhrajat al-ardu athqalaha is that NOTHING is truly buried or forgotten. We bury our dead, and we bury our secrets. We think, “That chapter is closed.” But the earth is PREGNANT with those secrets, and one day it will give BIRTH to them. The lesson: don’t assume time erases accountability. What you bury will be exhumed. What you hide will be revealed. Live accordingly.

When humans ask ma laha (what is wrong with it?), there’s deep wisdom in this question. It reveals that human beings instinctively know that this world SHOULD be stable, that chaos is not the original design. Our shock at catastrophe is actually a spiritual instinct—we remember Jannah, where nothing shook, where everything was peace. Our bewilderment at earthquakes is actually fitrah (primordial nature) crying out, “This isn’t how it’s supposed to be!” And we’re RIGHT—this world is temporary, flawed by design, and one day it will give way to the REAL world where la yara fiha ’iwajan wa-la amta (you will see in it no crookedness or deviation).

The earth speaking—tuhaddithu akhbaraha—teaches us about SHAHADA (witnessing). If the earth testifies, how much more will WE testify? Our hands, our feet, our skin—all will speak. The wisdom is this: your body is not just your tool; it’s your WITNESS. Treat it accordingly. Don’t use your hands for haram; they’ll testify against you. Don’t walk toward sin; your feet will testify. This creates a bodily ETHICS—every limb has dignity and will have a voice.

Bi-anna rabbaka awha laha contains the wisdom of DIVINE PERMISSION. Nothing happens—not even the earth’s testimony—without Allah’s authorization. This is profound: even JUSTICE on Judgment Day is not automatic or mechanical; it’s personally supervised by Allah. He INSPIRES the earth to speak. He GRANTS permission. This means Judgment Day is not a cold, impersonal process—it’s an intimate encounter between the Creator and His creation, with every detail under His direct watch. This should produce both reverence and hope.

Yawma’idhin yasduru an-nasu ashtata teaches that this life is a TEMPORARY GATHERING. We’re bunched together now—believer and disbeliever in the same family, same office, same neighborhood—but that’s not the ultimate reality. The ultimate reality is SEPARATION based on deeds. The wisdom: invest in the relationships that will last eternally (bonds of faith and righteousness), not just the ones that are circumstantial (bonds of blood or business that may mean nothing on Judgment Day).

Li-yuraw a’malahum reveals a merciful wisdom: Allah doesn’t just TELL you what you did; He SHOWS you. Why? Because human beings are visual. We need to SEE to believe. We need to SEE to fully grasp. Allah will make you WATCH a replay of your life. This is mercy—because when you see your sins played back, you can’t deny them. When you see your good deeds that you forgot, you’ll recognize Allah’s justice. The wisdom is that Allah’s judgment is so CLEAR, so UNDENIABLE, that even the guilty will ACCEPT their punishment, knowing it’s deserved.

Fa-man ya’mal mithqala dharratin khayran yarahu is perhaps the most HOPEFUL verse in the Quran. The wisdom: NEVER despair of Allah’s mercy. You think your good deeds are insignificant? Allah says even a SPECK is significant to Him. You smiled at someone—He saw it. You gave a dollar—He counted it. You suppressed anger for a moment—He recorded it. This teaches us that goodness is not measured by QUANTITY but by SINCERITY. A poor woman’s one dollar can outweigh a rich man’s million if her intention was purer and her sacrifice greater.

The wisdom of mentioning KHAYRAN (good) BEFORE sharran (evil) is pedagogical brilliance. Allah could have listed them in reverse order (evil then good), but He chose to mention GOOD first. Why? Because Allah wants to end on a note of HOPE, not despair. Even though the verse about evil comes second, the FIRST thing you hear is that your good deeds—even tiny ones—are preserved. This is Allah’s way of encouraging you: focus on accumulating good, not obsessing over past mistakes.

Wa-man ya’mal mithqala dharratin sharran yarahu contains the wisdom of DETERRENCE. If a speck of GOOD counts, then a speck of EVIL counts too. This closes the door on excuses: “It’s just a small lie,” “It’s just a little backbiting,” “It’s just a minor sin.” No—it’s a dharrah, and it will be weighed. The wisdom: develop SCRUPULOUSNESS (wara’)—avoid even the small sins, because they accumulate. A mountain is made of specks of dust; Hellfire is filled with “small” sins that people didn’t take seriously.

The parallelism of the final two verses teaches BALANCE. Islam is not a religion of despair (only focusing on punishment) nor is it a religion of complacency (only focusing on mercy). It’s BOTH: fa-man ya’mal khayran yarahu, wa-man ya’mal sharran yarahu. Do good—you’ll see it. Do evil—you’ll see it. This creates a balanced spirituality: HOPE in Allah’s mercy, FEAR of His justice.

The fact that Allah says yarahu (he will see it) rather than yujza bihi (he will be recompensed for it) is profound wisdom. Seeing your deeds is ITSELF a form of reward or punishment. For the righteous, seeing their good deeds will be JOY—“SubhanAllah, I forgot about that!” For the wicked, seeing their evil will be TORMENT—“Oh no, I thought I got away with that!” The wisdom: the greatest reward might not be Jannah’s palaces but the HONOR of seeing your good deeds counted; the greatest punishment might not be Hellfire’s flames but the SHAME of seeing your sins exposed.

The Surah’s brevity is itself wisdom. In just 8 verses, Allah summarizes the ENTIRE message of Judgment Day: earth’s upheaval, human shock, divine testimony, individual accountability, and perfect justice. This teaches that PROFOUND truths don’t require lengthy exposition. Sometimes the most powerful reminders are the SHORTEST. The Surah’s punch is in its conciseness—every word carries weight, just like every dharrah of deed carries weight.

Finally, the Surah teaches the wisdom of LIVING AS IF EVERY MOMENT COUNTS. If a speck is measured, then NO action is too small to matter. Smile—it’s charity. Remove harm from the road—it’s charity. Suppress anger—it’s jihad. Make dhikr while walking—it’s ibadah. The Surah transforms the mundane into the meaningful. Nothing is trivial when you realize yarahu—you will see it on Judgment Day. This creates a spirituality where EVERY moment is an opportunity, and EVERY choice has eternal consequences. This is the wisdom of mithqala dharratin: it sanctifies the ordinary and makes every second of life a chance to invest in forever.
